comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Many people who practice magic without banishing rituals note the attraction of strange and malicious beings.  I've heard several people call it 'light attracting dark.'  What do you think is going on here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There are at least three things going on.

First, once you begin practicing magic, you're going to start becoming aware of your nonphysical surroundings. Unless you happen to live in an area where the astral is unusually clean, that means you're going to become aware of the very cruddy astral environment in which you live, and the critters that normally inhabit such environments.

Second, the critters in question are, among other things, curious. If a human being suddenly starts doing magic, they're going to come padding around to see what you're up to.

Third, well, "light attracting dark" is a nice phrase, but a lot of people who take up magical practice aren't exactly shining examples of light, and neither are their practices. As Dion Fortune comments somewhere, genuine black magicians are rare but muddy gray ones are extremely common! If your magical workings are not very ethical, why, yes, you're going to attract critters.

comments.content: Might you be persuaded to give a nickel tour of this week's Fludd illustration?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is a simple one. The ladder has six rungs, which are labeled sensus (sensation), imaginatio (imagination), ratio (reason), intellectus (intellect), intelligentia (intelligence), and Verbum (the Word). We tend to munge reason, intellect, and intelligence together, but Fludd differentiated them as three powers of the mind: reason is the ability to think in a logical fashion, intellect is the ability to recognize the truth of a true statement, intelligence the ability to perceive truths intuitively without the need for discursive reasoning. He saw all these, plus imagination and sensation, as manifestations of the Christ force in human beings. The fine lines behind the ladder, btw, trace out his intersecting pyramids of spirit and matter.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you.  Why do simple drawings so easily hold such complex ideas?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have no idea. I know that they do, and that occultists have been using them for that purpose since the days when cave paintings were the latest hot communications technology, but why?  The Great Old Ones haven't told me. ;-)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: May I suggest that it is because, as non-verbal analogies, they circumvent the ratio and arrive at the intellectus directly, since analogy is one of the basic organizing principles of the Mental Plane, at which level the intellectus and intelligentia exists?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's certainly one way to think about it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Drawings render complex ideas in a spatial medium. Space is the dimension of the mental plane, so spatial images are very useful tools by which to express the meanings dwelling in complex thoughts.

Other types of images — emotional, creative, dream, experiential — tend to be more useful for unpacking the meanings embedded in things other than thoughts.

— Christophe

comments.title: Addiction
comments.content: Is there an occult explanation for the periodic (~hour) need for comfort? For myself this often manifests as self aggrandizing fantasies. For others I have seen food munching, drinking, smoking, music listening, and gossiping. I suspect substituting in affirmations and prayer would be beneficial and fulfill the need. Any thoughts on this from the occult perspective would be greatly appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Addiction
replies.content: I don't know of an occult explanation, but psychological?  Sure. People learn to do this as a way of distracting themselves from the miseries of their lives. Little pleasures are good for that. Yes, affirmations and prayer can fill the same purpose more constructively; you can also see if you can do something more constructive with your fantasy life -- if you've got a lively imaginative life, that's a powerful magical tool. You can use it for mini-pathworkings, for example, or simply construct a pleasant place in your imagination that has a lot of things that comfort you, and do magical practices there.

replies.content: Thank You.

replies.title: Re: Addiction
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Addiction

replies.title: Re: Addiction
replies.content:
"Formulate a symbol on an imaginary doorway. Pass through the doorway. See what's on the other side. Have a conversation with anything there that's interested. Return through the doorway. "

How does this work? More specifically, what benefits does this provide?

I'd think that in an untrained person,such a working would produce 'random static' from his psyche, and not so much information about the symbol itself. replies.title: Re: Addiction
replies.content:
Pathworking aka scrying has been being taught to complete beginners for a century or so, with tolerably good results. Of course your first experiences are going to include little of value, because the skills of imaginal work can only be developed through regular practice, but you can start now. Your goal is to develop a set of capacities for perception and action that most people don't have, or have only in a very weak and limited form.

As for your pathworking, good. That's a nice start. Meditate on what you perceived, trying to link everything you encountered back to hexagram 57. (You may not succeed, but make the attempt. How do night, moon, hills, lake, and the absence of wind relate to the hexagram and its traditional texts? Plan on at least three sessions of meditation for every pathworking. When you've gotten what you can, do it again.

comments.title: So how does the LBRP work for a beginner?
comments.content:
Hello James,

Context: I'm looking for a "path" to commit to, and the Golden Dawn system is one of the options I'm looking yet. I'm reading your Learning Ritual Magic book (great book! Thank You for writing it), but haven't started practising yet.

1) I'm curious as to how the LBRP actually works (even minimally) for a beginner? How does it "clear a space", more specifically, where does the power to do this come from? The beginning practitioner has no skills in magic, so the ritual must be doing it. Is the power (to banish, initially) drawn from the Golden Dawn egregore, till the practitioner develops enough power of his own?

2) As I understand, the LBRP the beginner practises, is the same as the pentagram that banishes the Earth element. Why is the Earth element (specifically) banished in the beginning? Why not (say) Fire, or Air?

I am a rank beginner, and these questions probably reflect that.

thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: So how does the LBRP work for a beginner?
replies.content:
Well, I'm not sure who James is, but I can certainly answer your questions. ;-)

1) The power that flows through the LBRP does not come from the individual who practices it. It comes from the divine names that are invoked in it. You can get much better at bringing through power from those sources, but the power isn't yours.

2) The Golden Dawn draws heavily from Christian tradition and therefore includes the Christian distrust of the earthy side of existence as one of its formative elements. (Remember that the world, along with the flesh and the Devil, are the three enemies of the Christian soul.) That's why earth is banished in the standard GD banishing ritual. In the Druidical GD, as discussed in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn, that's obviously not appropriate and so I had to work out a completely different way of handling the pentagrams; you can find a summary here.

replies.title: Re: So how does the LBRP work for a beginner?
replies.content:
Ha! sorry, John, not James. (Damn, I was *sure* your name was *James* Greer, I had to actually look at the cover of LRM just now!)

Thank you for these clear answers.

So the power of this ritual comes from Divinity as the GD conceives it, as reflected through the vibrated Hebrew names. Interesting!

CGD added to my reading list.

From your summary, you retain the pentagram symbolism (spirit + 4 elements), but use divine names from another pantheon, so the power comes from *those* deities. And you use clockwise for invoking, and ccw for banishing. That is a much cleaner design than the standard LBRP.

You've given me much to think about, for which I thank you!

replies.title: Re: So how does the LBRP work for a beginner?
replies.content: You're most welcome. Yes, the basic power source for all Golden Dawn ritual is divinity, not the individual self. That puts certain limitations on what you can do but it makes for extremely powerful magic.

replies.title: Re: So how does the LBRP work for a beginner?
replies.content:

What limitations does that remove? (My guess would be “alignment with the aims of the invoked entity”, at the cost of substantial limits on power and risk of overextension.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you've got floors you can mop, make up a bucket of the same mixture you'd use for a hoodoo bath -- hot water, salt, vinegar, and a little red pepper -- and mop the floors with it, starting in the kitchen and ending right inside the front door. This is called a floor wash and it's a standard hoodoo practice. If you're in a reall cruddy area, add a splash of Florida water to the mix.

If you have carpets, make a mix of salt and a little cayenne pepper, sprinkle it all over the carpets, leave it for a few hours or overnight, and then vacuum it up and empty the vacuum bag at once into your outside garbage.

comments.title: Request for Thoughts on Potential Encounter with a Goddess
comments.content:
Hello! Thank you again for doing this on a weekly basis, I've found it helpful as a lurker for a while, and now as an asker for a couple of weeks. I have a rather lengthy question, as the context seems necessary for it to make any sense, but I've given a short version if it turns that I'm wrong about the context being necessary, and this is something bog standard among new entrants to occultism.

Short version: if I had an unexpected, powerful, seemingly "ecstatic" experience of a goddess during discursive meditation, is it more likely I actually contacted that goddess, or that I worked myself into some kind of state?

Long version: I have been following your recommendations in the "Foundations of Magical Practice" series from the Well of Galabes for about three weeks now, after some earlier attempts at magical practice over the past few years where I have fallen off the wagon - I perform your Heathen Lesser Ritual of The Pentagram (Banishing), the Heathen Middle Pillar Exercise, a discursive meditation (on a single Rune, recently with the material from "Teutonic Magic", as recommended by another commenter here), and then a rune-casting. Yesterday, after meditating around 10 minutes or so on the rune "Berkana", which is associated with both the birth and death aspects of the feminine, I began to feel "tingly" all over my body, but especially in my chest and upper arms. I asked if someone was there and imagined a goddess. I then asked what she would like to be called, and "Idun" came to mind, and when I used that name, the tingly feeling intensified. I asked how she would like to be worshipped, and some not-exactly-tame mental images came to mind. All of these steps felt mentally the same as imagining something on my own, but the tingly feeling intensified in my chest and arms and I became light-headed - if asked to describe it, I would call it "ecstatic" - but since this was my first experience of the kind, I do not know whether that's how best to classify it.

Before I had gotten very far into the feeling/experience, the timer I had set for the meditation went off, and I straight away went outside to a lovely tree where I've said prayers and made offerings before and gave thanks to Idun and left her a piece of bread and some of a rather nice local IPA. My wife and daughter were getting ready to head to see a friend, and I helped them get ready and did some things around the house before getting to my rune-casting, which I usually do right after my discursive meditation. I felt good, and like I wanted to come back to what had happened, but mostly normal.

Already, this was maybe the strongest spiritual/religious experience I've had in my life, but after my wife and daughter left, I did a rune-casting that seemed to reflect the situation pretty well (the rune for "present" was Kenaz - energy/creativity/sexuality). After, I was putting the runestaves away and one fell, rolled under a nearby couch, and after some effort to fetch it, I found it was Ansuz - a rune with a meaning of "Ancestral God" that is most often associated with Woden/Odhinn. If nothing else, this was a striking element of synchronicity, so I immediately went outside to the same tree and gave thanks to Woden for his help in learning the runes and opening things spiritual to me and offered a cup of the same IPA.

During and right after the prayer/giving of the drink, I again felt tingly all over and felt as if Woden (or some of his presence, at least) was coming into me. From there, I went back inside and had an even stronger ecstatic experience that involved Woden coming into me and Idun coming to me/us, and well, going the way you might expect given the sexual mental imagery from earlier. Afterward, I found myself weeping with joy for a few minutes and with an irrepressible booming laugh for the next 15-30 minutes, right through a cold shower.

Since this was an enormously positive experience that occurred not long after I decided to try, as an experiment, thinking of the gods as genuinely and independently real, rather than as "only" very strong archetypes evolved into my brain (thanks in large part to A World Full of Gods), I'm inclined to count myself hugely lucky and to give all due respect and thanks to Woden and Idun for gracing me with such an experience.

That being said, until rather recently, I've been inclined to think of magic and religion in a Jungian "it's all psychological, but that's more powerful than most folks realize" framework. That kind of thinking leads me to some elements of potential skepticism: I did not "see" or "hear" anything that did not feel like "regular" imagination. I did feel some highly unusual somatic sensations and come away feeling happy and energetic, but I'm not experienced enough to know if that is what I would feel in the presence of a god or goddess. It happened while engaged in a known consciousness-altering practice (though without any fancy breathing or for any great length of time), but not one with the aim of contacting specific divine beings. The fact that arousal was involved also makes me suspicious that my imagination might just have been doing what I've been using my imagination to do since junior high, but in a newly elaborated and especially strong way - it didn't feel like any kind of sexual fantasizing I've ever done before, and the accompanying ecstatic feeling was rather different from only sexual climax (though that was there too). In terms of felt significance and strength of good feeling, I was reminded somewhat of the handful of times I've taken psychedelics, but with none of the perceptual hallucination, time dilation, or lack of normal mental clarity that mushrooms or LSD can bring.

If you've been kind enough to make it this far, any thoughts on how magically/religiously to think about this remarkable, somewhat lurid experience, described at such length?

Thanks very much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Request for Thoughts on Potential Encounter with a Goddess
replies.content:
What if it is only your imagination? How can you tell the difference?

For a little while now, I'll have meditations where an image or thought of a god will pop into my head out of nowhere, or they appear in SoP imagery. This might come with a feeling that I'm not sure how to describe: something in the air around me, a thickness, a pressure (it is sometimes visible, as a distortion or a light fog); sometimes it's just a clarity, like my mental faculties are much sharper than usual. But whenever I do follow up divination (tarot), on whatever minor revelations I got or about continuing on with the god, and it's always extremely negative. Like the thoughts were just my wandering mind, the physical/mental symptoms have some other cause, it wasn't a deity making contact, the deity (seemingly) doesn't even want contact.

I saw further down the page here, a comment you made to someone about negative thoughts getting in the way. Though this situation is different from the one below, I assume the same could be applying here as well? I know I have some lingering hang ups in this area, based on past experiences. But if so, what do I do with that? When my mind gives me one thing, and divination gives me another, and either could be possible, which do you go with?

replies.title: Re: Request for Thoughts on Potential Encounter with a Goddess
replies.content: The imagination is an interface between your consciousness and the consciousness of the universe. You can create images in it, and so can the universe. It can also happen that you create an image and something from the universe blends with it and ensouls it. That's one of the basic modalities of magic. "Only" your imagination!

replies.title: Re: Request for Thoughts on Potential Encounter with a Goddess
replies.content:
It might help to remember that even Christian mystics have ecstatic experiences which they speak of in erotic terms--St. Theresa of Avila springs immediately to mind. It seems even more likely that pagan Deities would approach humans using erotic imagery, considering how charged that imagery is for our species. It's not the only way to interact with us, of course, but I'm certain a lot more people have your kind of experiences than wish to speak about it publicly.

However, even if it was all in your imagination, what difference would it make? The Gods are real, whether we believe in Them or not, whether we ever experience Their presences or not. Either Idun and Woden chose you, or you chose Them for unconscious but important reasons. Regardless, the only polite and sensible thing to do is to develop relationship with Them and see what happens. You may or may not ever have such an intense encounter again, but I do hope you will come to know how loving the Gods means everything and changes everything.

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Request for Thoughts on Potential Encounter with a Goddess
replies.content: The Song of Solomon, part of the Biblical canon of all Christians and Jews, is a very erotic work, too.

replies.title: Re: Request for Thoughts on Potential Encounter with a Goddess
replies.content:

comments.title: Bitcoin Update
comments.content:
This is an update from two weeks ago when you brought to my attention the Bitcoin speculative bubble and recommended reading The Great Crash before proceeding to put any more money in. It took a while to get my money out with lots of technical difficulties, which turned out to be a huge relief that I did not wait for a death spiral to figure out the process. Much appreciated!

On Thursday I was ready to cash out but it looked like the price was climbing close to my break even point, so I decided to roll a chart and got FMaj & FMaj resolving in Populus, which upon looking back was clear indication that I was going to lose but that was the best result I could hope for. My monkey mind took over and I cannot remember how I justified not taking the deal, but the details do not really matter. Upon returning from exercise I found out about the Musk tweets and then hesitated for a bit while it floundered and then crashed again as Asia woke up, basically ruining the rest of my day and landing me a back spasm the next morning.

I am out some money, but it could have been way worse. More importantly I learned how easily I can delude myself, and will consider it a correspondence course in self awareness that will lead to more profitable decision making in the future.

In the meantime I decided to keep my hardware and occasionally check in for research (disregarding the misguided hype). Hermetix released a good podcast today that was heavy into the philosophy of Bitcoin, and I do believe that there is plenty of utility once the bear market is over. Very curious what this week's eclipse will bring, and I am extremely grateful to be sitting on the sidelines thanks to your suggestion.

replies.title: Re: Bitcoin Update
replies.content: Well, I'm glad you got out. It'll be interesting to see just how far things plunge when they finally get around to plunging.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I hope you’ll forgive a rather long and perhaps inexpert question, because I hope that other people might find any comments you have relevant as well.
Since retiring some years ago, I’ve kept myself busy doing intellectual work - teaching, lecturing, writing, translating etc. This earns me a little money but more importantly is a way of continuing to feel useful. A couple of years ago, for various reasons, the work started to tail off, and, with the virus, most of it has now gone. So earlier this year, I decided to invoke the help of Mercury to improve the situation and, for eight successive weeks, I made donations every Wednesday at the hour of Mercury to charities in different countries helping children to read. The sums were not great - about the price of an average meal. But nothing much has happened in the several weeks since I finished.
Although there were one or two small interesting coincidences, the situation has not improved, and one promising opportunity disappeared while I was making the donations. So I have been trying to work out what I did wrong, taking as a working assumption that normally TSW. It’s true I didn’t make a great ceremony of the donations with candles and invocations (my domestic circumstances precluded that). It’s also true that I didn’t ask for anything very precise - I was hoping for new ideas and possibilities as much as anything else. And I don’t know whether there was any confusion in offering to Mercury while working most of the time with the Egyptian pantheon, especially Thoth. And finally, an astrologer friend told me that I might have been suffering from the wider effects of several planets going retrograde earlier this year.
But I was sure that your response would be that I should meditate upon the lack of results, and I did so. I concluded that I was very conflicted about making the request in the first place. As a child, I learned that there was no point in asking for things, because there was no money in the family, so nothing would be forthcoming. I also grew up with the idea that anything I did receive, even of an intangible nature, would necessarily be at the expense of somebody else, since the overall store of goods and happiness in the world was finite. But depressing as that is to realise, I had thought it possible that the donations would have some effect, at least to a degree, independently of what I unconsciously thought about the process itself.
Finally, I tried geomancy recently, and received an answer which, in spite of working through your book, I don’t really understand. I asked simply « what effect will there be from the offerings I have made to Mercury ?» and received the following:

First House Conjunctio
Second House Albus
Third House Amissio
Fourth House Amissio
Fifth House Fortuna Major
Sixth House Laetitia
Seventh House Via
Eighth House Populus
Ninth House Rubeus
Tenth House Populus
Eleventh House Puella
Twelfth House Via
Judge Populus
First Witness Rubeus
Second Witness Rubeus
Reconciler Conjunctio

Do you have any thoughts?

Apologies once more for a rather long and complex question.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for this -- you've actually answered your own question, and it's an answer that applies to a lot of people. Successful magic requires a unified will, and when you're deeply conflicted about the subject of a magical working, it's not going to get far or do much. This is also true of making offerings to a deity -- remember, deities know what's going on in your head and your heart!

With regard to your geomantic chart, offerings to the gods belong to the 9th house of religion. There's no mode of perfection uniting Conjunctio in the 1st with Rubeus in the 9th, which gives a negative answer. Rubeus himself -- especially when he also appears as the witnesses, as here -- can indicate that you're not being entirely honest with yourself, or that there's some factor at work that you're not letting yourself take into account.

I might be at the edge of a breakthrough in terms of my own state of consciousness as a result of magical practice, but I also might be tricking myself.

We recently moved, and since then I've been quite anxious on and off about whether we made the right decision with the new place. There are a number of reasons for this, but throughout the experience I've noticed a few things:

- I realize that some of the factors I am worried about are external things that seem properly worthy of anxiety, because if we don't deal with them they will cause problems. Others, however, result from feelings about similar situations that reflect patterns I've experienced in the past. Then there are things in which both types are present: We have a neighbour in the new place we are worried about due to some worrying signs, but we are also tainted by very negative and difficult experiences with a neighbour in our old place. I realize it's unfair to judge the new neighbour on the basis of the latter type.

- As a result of this and other factors, I found myself over the past week quite often in a thick, emotionally-heavy, hazy state (that my early morning daily SOP does help banish for a while).

- Why I'm posting here today is because, unlike previous experiences of anxiety in past situations, I've noticed my mind telling me often something like "your own mind is creating this anxiety. It's a choice."

I am unsure whether that is my mind tricking me into repressing my emotional state (which I know never to do), or whether I am on the verge of consciously choosing not to be affected by the emotional state.

So I thought I'd ask for your advice! Sorry if my description is overly long, it's hard to describe because the experience is unfamiliar.

Thank you!

replies.content: Excellent! You're in the process of learning one of the great lessons of life, which is that 90% of the suffering human beings experience in life is the result of their own attitudes. Anxiety, in particular, is never a helpful habit.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
- Do you have any advice if I'm the type of person who relatively easily understands knowledge intellectually, but has trouble putting it into practice when it comes to applying this knowledge to everyday life? I have been helped greatly through my studies of the occult, daily ritual and meditation. But the learnings from this fly right out the window in my social interactions, or at work, for example. He is filled with great bitterness about "humanity's treatment of the environment and the dying planet", and likely has depression. It's to the point where he now thinks there should be a divide between types of people: those who are "conscious" and those who aren't. By conscious he means aware of their connectedness to the whole of nature. He also doesn't believe that he has the capacity for evil, that he doesn't have a dark side.

I gently reminded him, which I do every time we have these conversations, that very rarely do people think that they do have such a dark side, that he should look to his own lifestyle to make environmental changes (he's firmly in the upper middle class), and that in a complex universe, it is impossible for humans to understand it, and therefore claims of superior consciousness are relative, all that one can do is have a sense of humility in the face of this unknowable cosmos. I sometimes sort of poke through, but the fact that he keeps repeating the same arguments each time we meet shows that something else is at work, reasoning with him isn't working.

I'm not sure how to help him, and whether he can even be helped. He doesn't know I pursue occult studies, so I have been carefully and subtly also trying to point him in that direction, as it sounds like he could use some kind of spiritual path. Do you have any advice?

- Lastly, in the same conversation, one of the points I gently touched on was reincarnation. He dismissed the idea of reincarnation as being 'the ego's desire for continuity and perpetuation through eternity' (I paraphrase). I couldn't go on to explain that in my understanding of reincarnation, based on the Druid Revival teachings, is that the ego/personality vanishes after death, so his point is moot. However, he did make me ponder the point further. Have you run into this line of thinking before?

Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) This is very common. You're changing habits that you've spent a lifetime building, and that your society has spent that same lifetime pressing on you. Be patient with yourself and do what you can, when you can.

2) When you see someone getting all bent out of shape about the awful things those awful people over there are doing, while denying any evil at all in himself, you're seeing what Jung called "projection of the Shadow." Your relative doesn't want to deal with the mismatch between his behavior and his ideals, and so projects that onto other people, convincing himself that they're bad so that he can feel virtuous by comparison. People who get into Shadow projection typically have deep emotional investment in it and can't be talked out of it, since that would require them to face up to their own failure to live up to their ideals. Until and unless he's ready to grow up, there's not much you can do.

3) It's quite common. In my experience, at least, it usually covers over the ego's desire not to have to continue, and thus not to have to deal with the consequences of its actions...

comments.title: Mage v. Mystic
comments.content: Hi JMG. For awhile now I have been pondering a question that has appeared on this forum before—the difference between a mage and a mystic. The last time I recall seeing this topic discussed here, there was no definitive answer to the question. Perhaps I missed it.  Anyway, after thinking more about it since then, the conclusion I have come to is that a mage is one who, through various practices, attempts to change consciousness according to the self’s (i.e., mage’s own) will, whereas a mystic, through various practices, attempts to change consciousness in accordance with a deity’s (i.e., divine) will.  Am I on the right track here? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Mage v. Mystic
replies.content:
No, not really. Many mages work very closely with deities.

The difference between the mage and the mystic is the direction in which energy moves. In all things there's a current of energy that descends from the divine into manifestation, and a current of energy that ascends from manifestation toward the divine. The mage works with the descending current, bringing forces down from the divine into manifestation, and if he knows what's he's doing, he uses divination and other means to do so in harmony with the divine purpose. The mystic works with the ascending current, aspiring upwards toward a personal union with the divine and carrying part of the karma of humanity with him.

replies.title: Re: Mage v. Mystic
replies.content:
Thank you for this! This distinction will definitely provide some fodder for a few meditations :)

replies.title: Re: Mage v. Mystic
replies.content: There's no harm at all. Most people tend one way or the other.

comments.title: Auras?
comments.content:
Hullo JMG,

I hope you are well, and happy birthday!

I remember a few years ago I was first getting into Buddhist meditation, I started to see auras around things and people when I was highly concentrated, and "looking" in a particular way.

It's still difficult for me to do this, though I've definitely had experiences with it that indicate to me that it is reflective of something real. (Affected by people's emotional states, etc)

So I have two questions, I'll start with the more simple one.

1.

Often when I meditate in front of my Ovate CGD altar, after looking at the cross and the circle for a long time, they switch colors in Their "after images" for lack of a better term. These after images seem burned into my retinas after looking for a long in the same way light is after looking at a bright light. The cross's after image is green and the circles is red.

I've heard a "scientific" explanation of this, that the eyes simply grow fatigued looking at surfaces of a color for a long time, then the retinas show their opposite color. Is this what this experience is only? Or should I attempt to meditate upon it and discover further symbolism or occult significance?

Is this similar, the same as, or different to seeing auras around people? They have a similar visual quality, though at this time I tend to think they're somewhat different.

2.

I would often see Auras in classes in college, when someone was lecturing. I think this is because I would be intently focused on a single figure against a somewhat blank, often white background. And perhaps people lecturing are more "projective" and focused as well.

Anyway, I noticed that sometimes when focusing directly on someone in this way, making a lot of eye contact, paying close attention to everything they're saying, the lecturer would sometimes pick me out of the crowd, and sometimes lose their train of thought, or stop and look away before continuing. During those moments, sometimes I would feel embarrassed and look away, or the lecturer would, but I often felt like a more significant connection was started or made during those moments of eye contact. But it often seemed to interrupt the lecturers train of thought.

Perhaps a mundane explanation would be the person thought, "Man! That bald guys looking at me way too intensely!" And it simply distracted them, but I wonder if there's not more going on there because of this experience I'll now relate.

I was in an all-staff meeting for my job at the time, and fighting boredom I was trying to pay tons of attention and focus very closely on what one of the boss people,
(who I emotionally reacted to as if he was too controlling, or a jerk, though I had few interactions with him so I have no idea how true or false that is)
I noticed earlier in the meeting, his aura was red, strong, it felt brash and vaguely angry to me, he then began to talk about how he was retiring, and his aura turned blue, and more clear.

Then he made eye contact with me, and stopped talking for what felt like 2-3 seconds, he seemed out of sorts for a moment and while he was looking at me his aura turned back red.

Then he regained his bearings , looked away, and continued talking.

I don't know if I was unintentionally doing something subtle or occult to these people with my intense focus that seemes to pull people out of their train of thought, or if I just have a really weird concentration face!

Do you have any info, thoughts, or pointers about what could be happening here?
Is this normal social dynamics, but I'm just paying more attention now?

Thank you for your time, especially on your birthday!

-WindMan

replies.title: Re: Auras?

The alternate color that you see is partly physical and partly astral. The Golden Dawn used to have people stare at colored shapes for a long while and then look at a blank wall, and use the afterimage as the starting point for scrying. As for the focus effect, European occultists call that the central gaze, and use it to disrupt certain kinds of hostile magic. Yes, you're doing something occult.

replies.title: Re: Happy BDay, DA Update, and Non-Herd Writing
replies.content:
Back when I was a teenager and attended science fiction conventions -- those two were not unrelated -- I got to hear a lecture by the editor of Isaac Asimov's Science Fiction Magazine, George Scithers. One of the many things he said turned out to be the most useful piece of advice I ever received. It was this: "Every would-be writer has about a million words of bad writing stuck inside his brain. The only way to get them out, and get down to the good stuff, is to write them."

If you're bored by your writing, good. Try writing a story, setting it aside for a few weeks, and then editing it into a completely different style. If you like long run-on sentences, as I do, revise it to sound like Hemingway. If you prefer to write like Hemingway, revise it to sound like Faulkner. Read classic works of fiction and nonfiction and see if you can figure out what the writer is doing that impresses you so much. Read really, really awful fiction and then make a list of every mistake the writer made. Now do the same thing with your worst story -- and with your best. And keep writing.

comments.title: Astrology and COVID Vaccination
comments.content:
The recent discussions regarding divinations on COVID and the vaccines have had me curious and concerned. Seeing as how I already had my first dose of M*d*rn*, I decided to look at the charts for both that appointment, and the second dose appointment, which has yet to happen. I suppose I'm wondering if the tampering can extend to interpretations of the heavens. Both are in Placidus.

1st Dose: https://i.imgur.com/YwdgA5G.jpg

2nd Dose: https://i.imgur.com/DlenBT3.jpg

My research says that a vaccine would be represented by the 10th House, which in the first chart is ruled by Mars. That puts two things immediately to mind: inflammation, which has been brought up already today, and the general "combat the virus" nature of vaccines. The fact that Mars is in the 12th House and in his fall suggests a more negative interpretation, but I don't know if that's ameliorated with him being so close to the ascendant (I've heard it said that Ptolemy suggested a planet within 5 degrees of a house cusp acts as if it's within the following house) and in mutual reception with the Moon in Scorpio by trine. That should provide some support, I think? But they're both in a grand trine with Neptune in the 9th House, which is also suggesting illusions/idealism regarding the science. Mars is also opposing Pluto, which I think just kind of... describes the whole COVID and vaccination situation.

Any sort of silver lining I found in the first chart seems dimmer in the second chart. The vaccine in this chart is represented by Venus in the 12th House, without any proximity to the ascendant. Her presence with Mars in Leo in the 12th House seems to speak more to inflammation (that intense, hot and dry nature). They both benefit from a trine to an angular Moon, who rules the 12th House. They're also both opposing Pluto and Saturn in the 6th House, which generally describes the nature of the disease at play. So- maybe the vaccine does what it's supposed to in combatting COVID, which started with that mess of Pluto and Saturn in 2019, but it seems to come with a potential for inflammation, at least in my case, as far as these charts apply to me and my situation.

My interpretations feel very basic, and I would appreciate if any course corrections came immediately to mind in terms of interpretations. I hope it at least adds a little more data to the situation.

I honestly feel very hesitant to get the second dose and trigger the intense immune reaction.

Best,

Jean-Pierre.

replies.title: Re: Astrology and COVID Vaccination
replies.content:
Okay, first question -- why do you identify vaccines with the 10th?

I'd look to the 6th in a chart of this kind, since that's the house that rules health. In the first chart, the ruler of the 6th is in the 8th afflicted by a square with the Sun, not a good sign, though the Part of Fortune is in the 6th, which indicates good luck. In the second chart, the ruler of the 6th is malefic Saturn in the 6th, square Uranus and the midheaven and opposite Mars: a really negative sign. This latter is the kind of chart that in medical astrology heralds a serious illness.

replies.title: Re: Astrology and COVID Vaccination
replies.content:
I was reading Deborah Houlding's "The Houses: Temples of the Sky," which assigns the 10th House to "the cure; whether the medicine will succeed in overcoming the illness." I figured that's what a vaccine is supposed to be, since there wasn't an assignment specifically for vaccinations.

I suppose the luck in my situation is partly taking this astrological look before the second dose, but that reading of the second chart does solidify my suspicions.

Aside from my beginning-level interpretations, it's not a far stretch that anything or anyone tampering with divinations on these matters might tamper with the consciousness of persons performing interpretations on charts? My initial thought was that what was being affected in other forms of divination was the "randomness" of the symbols being drawn; they couldn't affect the positions of the planets.

comments.title: Awakening the Solar Plexus
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I’m working through the ‘Using the Solar Plexus’ exercises in Gradd Yr Ymarferiwr of the DA, and I have a few questions:

1. “The negative state of each of the first six Cantrefs is countered by the positive attitude of the Cantref opposite to it.” Is this its opposite in terms of how it is positioned in the SoP? So the First Cantref is opposite to the Second Cantref; the Third Cantref is opposite to the Fourth Cantref; and the Fifth Cantref is opposite to the Sixth Cantref?

I’m autistic, and emotionally overwhelming situations can quite easily lead to meltdowns. I’ve found that when I imagine the green energy of the Fourth Cantref flowing through my body from the solar plexus, I can interrupt the escalation of an overwhelming situation from becoming a full blown meltdown, which, for me, usually includes self-injurious behaviour. As I’m sure you can imagine, this has been a life-changing discovery and practice. Thank you so much for making it available.

2. I often communicate in a very literal way, and this can lead to feelings of intense frustration with my partner when her style of communication includes space to ‘read between the lines’ of meaning. Would frustration be classified as an aspect of anger, and so another negative state of the Third Cantref?

3. I also notice myself being judgemental of the behaviour of others, especially when their actions don’t make ‘logical’ sense to my way of thinking. Would this judgement be an aspect of pride, and so a negative state of the First Cantref?

Thank you so much!

replies.title: Re: Awakening the Solar Plexus
replies.content:
This is all excellent, the kind of work I liked to see when I was teaching the Dolmen Arch as a course. Thank you for taking it seriously!

1) Yes, exactly. It's opposite in terms of the placement in the SoP.

As you may know, I have Aspergers syndrome; my meltdowns fortunately don't lead to self-injury, but they're miserable enough. Thus I'm delighted that you've been able to use the Dolmen Arch methods to help keep your balance.

2 & 3) Yes, exactly. You can balance out each one with its opposite.

Hi...I’m the recovering Unitarian from last week. Thanks for your reply by the way.

Is there anything I can do to improve my ability to hear the gods or otherwise receive communication?

Or do you think it’s ever possible that the gods just don’t want to have anything to do with someone? My past might make it difficult but could it make them turn away from me somehow?

Are there any pantheons or gods that are on the whole easier than others in cases like this?

Thanks again!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, relax. If the gods really want to tell you something they'll make sure you hear it.

Second, gods vary in their attitudes toward human beings. Some of them couldn't care less about us. Others find us endearing. There might be some who aren't interested in you but it's unlikely that it's anything you did. (Do you hold grudges against one-year-olds?)

Third, if you're interested in interacting with gods, and don't have a clear sense yet as to which gods you want to interact with, my suggestion is to spend some time reading books of mythology from many different parts of the world. That's an excellent idea anyway, because it can help pop you out of the very narrow and stifling habits of thinking of our culture, but you may also find that one set of myths or one deity is especially appealing to you. If that happens, prayer is your next step; we can talk about that when you get there. (No, it doesn't involve groveling. Do you remember God in Monty Python and the Holy Grail? "Well, stop it. If there's one thing I can't stand, it's groveling." A lot of deities are like that.)
